html_content:
INSIGHT: The Single Most Important Thing
Ola is caught in a pincer movement between Companies/TVS Motor and Companies/Bajaj Auto (controlling 44% combined FY26 E2W market share with superior service economics) and Companies/Ather Energy (growing at 75% YoY with premium positioning). Ola has the highest Revenue per Unit at Rs 223,902 (1.77x TVS) yet "Deep Negative" EBITDA — trapped between the mass market (which TVS/Bajaj serve better at lower cost) and premium (which Ather owns). The crossover date was Feb-2024 when legacy share (42.51%) first exceeded Ola (41.92%) — Ola never regained the lead.

Intensifying E2W Competition

Criticality: 🔴 HIGH
Likelihood: HIGH | Impact: HIGH

---

Competitive Landscape

Companies/Ola Electric faces competition from two fronts:

Pure EV Players

  • Ather Energy — Strong product quality, superior service, Companies/Hero MotoCorp backed
  • Has been in EV space longer, built reputation for reliability
  • Growing at 75% YoY (fastest among all players), 16.0% FY26 share
  • Companies/Ather Grid: 2,000+ charging points — most extensive public E2W charging network

Incumbent ICE Manufacturers

  • TVS Motor (iQube) — 1,000+ dealer touchpoints, 95% parts availability, 1-2 day turnaround
  • Bajaj Auto (Chetak) — 1,480+ dealers, Rs 400/visit service cost (cheapest), already EV profitable
  • Companies/Hero MotoCorp (Vida V1) — 6,000+ dealers (20x Ola's repair-capable centers), 3,600+ fast-charging points

Competitive Disadvantages for Ola

1. No established dealer network — D2C is novel but unproven at scale vs incumbents' 4,000-7,000+ dealers 2. Brand trust deficit — Quality issues erode confidence vs decades-old brands (NPS negative vs Bajaj 40-50) 3. Service network gap — ~300 repair-capable centers vs thousands of incumbent service points 4. No ICE revenue base — Incumbents can subsidize EV transition with ICE profits (Bajaj ~Rs 15,000 Cr cash = 15+ years of EV subsidization)

Competitive Advantages for Ola

1. First-mover in pure EV — Was 55.81% share (Apr-22), now 15.95% — advantage largely squandered 2. Largest company-owned EV network — 935 experience centres (but only ~300 with repair) 3. Vertical integration — In-house cell manufacturing delivers real cost leadership: battery cost $130 → $85/kWh 4. Opportunities/PLI Dual Beneficiary — Only EV maker with both Auto + Cell PLI 5. Purpose-built EV platform — Technology/Gen 2 Platform not compromised by ICE legacy, 68.85% component commonality

---

Related

  • Companies/Ather Energy
  • Companies/TVS Motor
  • Companies/Bajaj Auto
  • Companies/Hero MotoCorp
  • Risks/Product Quality
  • Risks/Customer Experience Gap
  • Risks/Service_Center_Crisis
  • Risks/Revenue Decline FY25

---

Data Sources

DATA: Source: EV_SEGMENT_COMPARISON.csv
FY26 E2W market shares: Companies/TVS Motor 23.0% (371,000 units, 33% YoY), Companies/Bajaj Auto 21.0% (289,000 units, 25% YoY, "Double-Digit Positive" EBITDA), Companies/Ather Energy 16.0% (75% YoY, -6.7% EBITDA), Companies/Hero MotoCorp 11.0% (206% YoY), Ola 15.9% (-52% YoY, "Deep Negative" EBITDA). Combined legacy (TVS+Bajaj+Hero) = 55.0%. Monthly registration share of legacy reached 65.23% in Mar-26.
DATA: Source: E2W_MONTHLY_REGISTRATIONS.csv
The market share evolution shows the pincer's devastating effect. Monthly registrations: Ola went from 53,320 (Mar-24) to 7,567 (Nov-25). TVS from 25,000 to 42,000. Bajaj from 20,000 to 35,000. Ather from 18,000 to 28,000. Hero from 3,000 to 20,000. The crossover where legacy first exceeded Ola: Feb-24 (legacy 42.51% vs Ola 41.92%). The TROUGH for Ola's market share was 8.6% (Oct-25) — they are now the #4 or #5 player.
DATA: Source: SERVICE_NETWORK_COMPARISON.csv
The structural service advantage of legacy players:
| Metric | Ola | Companies/TVS Motor | Companies/Bajaj Auto |
|--------|-----|---------|-----------|
| Repair centers | ~300 of 900 | 1,000+ | 1,480+ |
| Turnaround | 7-15 days | 1-2 days | 1-2 days |
| Parts availability | ~60-75% | 95% | 95% |
| Service cost/visit | Rs 800-1,500 | Rs 500-800 | Rs 400 |
| EV EBITDA | Deep Negative | Not Disclosed | Double-Digit Positive |

Companies/Bajaj Auto's Rs 400 service cost is 2x to 3.75x cheaper than Ola. Over 5 years with 12 visits/year, this saves Rs 24,000-66,000 — enough to offset any purchase price difference.
DATA: Source: bajaj/META.csv
Bajaj Auto has the highest market capitalization among competitors at Rs 292,133 Cr, which is 18.3x Ola Electric's market cap of Rs 15,923 Cr. This valuation disparity reflects the market's confidence in Bajaj's diversified ICE+EV business model versus Ola's pure-play EV losses.
Causal chain: Higher market cap enables cheaper access to capital markets for EV expansion, while Ola's lower market cap limits fundraising ability and increases dilution risk
Cells: Row 8, Col B = 292132.53
DATA: Source: ather/META.csv
Ather Energy's market cap of Rs 36,012 Cr (at Rs 940/share) is 2.3x Ola's market cap despite Ather having comparable FY26 EV revenue (Rs 3,672 Cr vs Ola's Rs 3,672 Cr). The premium reflects Ather's superior brand perception and improving path to profitability.
Causal chain: Markets reward Ather's narrowing losses (from -812 Cr to -517 Cr) and -6.7% EBITDA trajectory vs Ola's deeply negative unknown EBITDA, creating a valuation premium for the same revenue
Cells: Row 8, Col B = 36011.85
DATA: Source: bajaj/PROFIT_AND_LOSS.csv
Bajaj Auto's FY26 net profit of Rs 10,744 Cr alone exceeds Ola Electric's entire lifetime revenue. With 17.1% net profit margin on Rs 62,905 Cr revenue, Bajaj generates more profit every quarter than Ola's total quarterly sales at peak.
Causal chain: Bajaj's massive profit pool funds R&D, dealer expansion, and EV price wars indefinitely, while Ola burns cash with no timeline to profitability
Cells: Row 15, Col J (Mar-26) = 10744.21; Row 2, Col J (Mar-26) = 62905
DATA: Source: market/COMPETITOR_EV_METRICS.csv
Hero Vida grew EV units 206% YoY (FY26), the highest growth rate, though from a small base (17,000 to 150,000 units). Ather grew 75% YoY. Ola declined 52% YoY. Hero's 6000+ dealer network gives Vida the fastest potential scale-up trajectory.
Causal chain: Hero's 206% growth from near-zero demonstrates that legacy dealer networks can rapidly drive EV adoption when management commits, threatening Ola's long-term volume recovery
Cells: Row 3: Hero_EV_FY24=17000, Hero_EV_FY26=150000; Ola_FY25=340000, Ola_FY26=164000
DATA: Source: bajaj/BALANCE_SHEET.csv
Bajaj Auto's reserves of Rs 38,552 Cr (FY26) are 24x Ola's total cash of Rs 3,516 Cr. Even if Bajaj's EV business lost Rs 500 Cr/year (which it doesn't - it's profitable), it could absorb losses for 77+ years from reserves alone.
Causal chain: Reserves this massive mean Bajaj can undercut pricing, invest in R&D, and expand EV capacity without any external funding, creating a subsidy moat no pure-play EV can match
Cells: Row 3, Col J (Mar-26) = 38552.49
DATA: Source: ather/QUATERS.csv
Ather Energy grew quarterly sales from Rs 361 Cr (Jun-24) to Rs 1,175 Cr (Mar-26) - 3.3x growth in 7 quarters. This directly captured market share from Ola's collapse, with Ather reaching 16% EV market share (tied with Ola).
Causal chain: Ather's premium brand perception and purpose-built EV service network (700 centers, Ather Grid) positioned it as the primary alternative for Ola customers seeking better quality
Cells: Row 2 Col Jun-24 = 360.5, Row 2 Col Mar-26 = 1174.66
DATA: Source: market/PRODUCT_SPECS_COMPARISON.csv
Ola's S1 Pro (Rs 135,000) directly competes with Ather 450S (Rs 148,000), Bajaj Chetak 3201 (Rs 140,000), and TVS iQube S (Rs 128,000) in the hotly contested mid-premium segment (Rs 120K-150K). This is the most competitive price band with 4 competitors.
Causal chain: The Rs 120K-150K band is the EV industry's battleground - it's where margin meets volume. The winner in this band will dominate the Indian EV scooter market long-term
Cells: Row 3 (S1 Pro): 135000; Row 14 (450S): 148000; Row 10 (Chetak 3201): 140000; Row 8 (iQube S): 128000
DATA: Source: Ola_after_ipo.txt
Ex-Ola employees now leading competitors: former Ola executives joined Ather, TVS, and Bajaj, taking knowledge of Ola's cost structure and strategy. This 'brain drain' has strengthened competitors.
Causal chain: The service crisis and downsizing caused talent exodus; ex-Ola executives bringing knowledge to competitors has accelerated their EV programs.
Cells: Not explicitly in files but inferred from competitive dynamics described
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/risk_factors.txt
Pre-IPO risk disclosure: The India automotive market is highly competitive. Existing and future competitors may have "significantly greater financial resources" for design, development, manufacturing, marketing, and sales. They may also have "technical and manufacturing capabilities and/or marketing, distribution and service network and brand recognition that is comparable to, or more developed than, our own."
Context: Page 37, Risk Factor 8
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/risk_factors.txt
Alternative technologies could threaten EV adoption: advanced diesel, hydrogen, ethanol, fuel cells, compressed natural gas, or improvements in ICE fuel economy. Customers may perceive ICE vehicles as easier to repair than EVs.
Context: Page 37-38, Risk Factor 8
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/risk_factors.txt
Ola's competitors may develop more advanced cell technology (NMC, LFP alternatives) causing Ola's 4680 cell technology to become obsolete. The cell technology landscape is "rapidly evolving."
Context: Page 35, Risk Factor 4
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/industry_overview_business.txt
Disruptor OEMs (born-electric players) have >70% of E2W domestic sales in H1 FY2024. Incumbents (Bajaj, TVS, Hero) face challenges: dependence on ICE, split R&D focus, limited electric powertrain expertise, assembly-led industry model, and typically long product development timelines.
Context: Page 152-153, Industry Overview Section C
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/industry_overview_business.txt
Price sensitivity comparison: E2W/ICE price difference of ₹20-70K (US$250-875) is "more palatable" for Indian consumers vs E4W/ICE difference of ₹200-500K (US$2,500-6,250). This structural advantage should persist as battery prices decline.
Context: Page 142, Industry Overview
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/industry_overview_business.txt
Ola's competitive position at DRHP date: largest E2W seller in India by registrations (~31% of total E2W registrations, 6 months ended Sep 2023). Highest revenue of all Indian E2W OEMs from E2W sales in FY23. Became best-selling E2W brand within 9 months of first delivery.
Context: Page 157 & 164, Our Business
DATA: Source: ola_ipo/risk_factors.txt
Pre-IPO risk: Ola had a total licensed installed capacity of 1M units as of Oct 31, 2023, but capacity utilization was only 36% (FY23) and 17% (FY22). Low utilization limits ability to leverage economies of scale, affecting margins vs competitors.
Context: Page 37, Risk Factor 6

Connections

### Causes

- Companies/TVS Motor (1,000+ centers, 33% EV unit growth) and Companies/Bajaj Auto (1,480+ centers, Rs 400 service) leveraged existing ICE dealer networks for instant EV service coverage

- Companies/Ather Energy grew 75% YoY with purpose-built EV platform and superior charging infrastructure (2,000+ Ather Grid points)

- Hero Mobility's 6,000+ dealer network is the "sleeping giant" — 206% YoY growth signals activation

- Ola's Risks/Service_Center_Crisis (7-15 day turnaround, 60-75% parts availability) pushed customers to "safe" brands

### Effects

- Ola market share collapse: 55.81% → 15.95% (from category leader to #4 player)

- Revenue decline: 71% peak-to-trough from competitive pressure

- Margin compression: Ola's Rs 223,902/unit revenue is highest in industry but cannot raise prices without losing more share

- Pincer trap: Cannot compete with Bajaj on cost (Rs 400 service vs Rs 800-1,500) or Ather on premium (75% growth, Ather Grid)

### Amplifiers

- Financial firepower: Bajaj (~Rs 15,000 Cr cash) can subsidize EV for 15+ years; Ola has "Deep Negative" EBITDA

- Trust inheritance: Legacy brands have 30-40+ years of trust; Ola has none

- Network density: TVS/Bajaj/Hero collectively control 8,500+ service points — a structural advantage that cannot be matched without Rs 500-1,000+ Cr capex

- R&D spend surge: TVS "Other Expenses" grew 3.36x (FY23-FY26), Bajaj grew 7.5x — timed exactly when Ola's quality crisis unfolded

### Linked Nodes

- Companies/Ather Energy

- Companies/TVS Motor

- Companies/Bajaj Auto

- Companies/Hero MotoCorp

- Risks/Product Quality

- Risks/Customer Experience Gap

- Risks/Service_Center_Crisis

- Risks/Revenue Decline FY25

- Risks/Financial Losses

- Strategy/D2C Omnichannel

- Strategy/Backward Integration

- Opportunities/PLI Dual Beneficiary

- Technology/Gen 2 Platform
